stories.headline: Metro Denver inflation hits 5% in May, highest rate since 2023, driven by energy costs
stories.summary:
Metro Denver's Consumer Price Index rose 5% between May 2025 and May 2026, the highest inflation rate the region has seen since September 2023, according to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Energy costs surged 25.3% over that period, including a 41.8% jump in gasoline prices tied to the ongoing conflict with Iran, accounting for roughly 60% of the overall inflation increase.

Food costs climbed 1.8% year-over-year in Denver, with fruits and vegetables up 4%. Clothing costs rose 13.9% locally versus 4.8% nationally, and alcoholic beverage prices jumped 8% in Denver compared with just 2.1% nationally, reflecting the compounding effects of higher tariffs and shipping costs.

On the positive side, rents in metro Denver showed only a modest 0.2% increase, consistent with broader market data showing declining rents across the region. However, an equivalent shelter cost measure for homeowners rose 4.2%, and 30-year mortgage rates have climbed back into the mid-6% range after briefly dipping below 6% earlier in the year.

Colorado State University economist Phyllis Resnick said the consumer is "increasingly vulnerable" and predicted the state's economic outlook will weaken further unless conditions change. Denver's 5% inflation rate outpaced the 4.2% national figure and the 3.5% pace measured across the Western region.

stories.headline: Six candidates vie for Colorado attorney general as June 30 primary approaches
stories.summary:
With the June 30 primary weeks away, six candidates — three Democrats and three Republicans — are competing to succeed two-term Attorney General Phil Weiser, who is term-limited. Secretary of State Jena Griswold leads all candidates in both name recognition and fundraising, having raised nearly $1.9 million through late May, nearly twice the amount raised by the second-highest fundraiser in the race.

Griswold, 41, has centered her campaign on opposing the Trump administration, highlighting her record on election security and her efforts to remove Trump from Colorado's 2024 ballot. Her legal resume has come under scrutiny from primary opponents, who note she has less courtroom litigation experience than other candidates in the field.

Democratic candidate David Seligman, executive director of the labor rights law firm Towards Justice, has positioned himself at the left edge of the field with a focus on immigration enforcement and economic inequality. University of Colorado Boulder law professor Douglas Spencer noted that the attorney general's office, with its law-and-order focus, represents the most realistic opportunity for Republicans to reclaim a statewide seat in Colorado's increasingly Democratic political landscape.

Before Weiser's election in 2018, Republicans held the attorney general's office for 14 consecutive years under Cynthia Coffman and John Suthers. The winner of the November general election will inherit an office increasingly defined by its role in multi-state legal battles against federal policy.

stories.headline: Avalanche GM Joe Sakic says Cale Makar 'going to finish his career' in Colorado, extension a top priority
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche president of hockey operations and general manager Joe Sakic said Thursday that signing two-time Norris Trophy-winning defenseman Cale Makar to a contract extension is his top offseason priority. Sakic, who recently took over GM duties after Chris MacFarland departed for Nashville, said negotiations with Makar's agent are already underway and he expects a deal to be completed this summer.

Makar, 27, has one year remaining on a six-year, $54 million contract signed in 2021 and becomes eligible to sign an extension on July 1. He finished the regular season with 79 points in 75 games and was named the league's best defenseman for the second time, having also won the award in 2022 when Colorado captured the Stanley Cup.

Sakic acknowledged the team's disappointment after being swept by the Vegas Golden Knights in the Western Conference Final despite finishing with 121 points — the most in franchise history — and winning the Presidents' Trophy. He said the core group remains motivated and that Colorado's championship window is still open.

"We got a great hockey team here, and this team was built for a two-, three-year run," Sakic said. "We still got most of the guys coming back, and their expectations, our expectations, I know the fans' expectations, is to try and win a Cup."

stories.headline: Broncos sign head coach Sean Payton to new five-year contract extension
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos announced Thursday that they have signed head coach Sean Payton to a new five-year contract extension, securing the architect of the team's recent turnaround through the foreseeable future. Owner and CEO Greg Penner cited Payton's role in building a winning culture and elevating expectations within the organization.

The extension comes during a busy offseason for Denver, with Payton having recently handed play-calling duties to quarterbacks coach Davis Webb, 31, who emerged as one of the hottest head coaching candidates in the league this past winter. Webb had interviews with the Buffalo Bills, Baltimore Ravens and Las Vegas Raiders before Denver retained him by expanding his responsibilities.

Webb said Thursday that Denver remains a "Sean Payton-coached football team" operating under Payton's offensive philosophy, but indicated he intends to bring a faster pace and different energy to the unit. He pointed to operational speed as a key area for improvement after the offense ranked 14th in the league last season and quarterback Bo Nix was visibly frustrated at times by slow play-calling from the sideline.

"Our whole deal is, 'Let's score some points, let's have some fun, let's have a different attitude on offense,'" Webb told reporters. Payton's extension signals the organization's long-term commitment to the structure he has built since arriving in Denver.

stories.headline: Metro Denver air quality advisory issued through Friday due to atmospheric ozone intrusion
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment issued an air quality health advisory Thursday covering much of the Front Range, including Denver, Boulder, Jefferson, southern Weld, and parts of Douglas, Arapahoe and Adams counties. Officials cited an "intrusion of ozone from high up in the atmosphere to the surface" as the cause, with elevated levels expected to persist through at least 4 p.m. Friday.

State officials also issued an Ozone Action Day Alert for the Front Range urban corridor stretching from Douglas County in the south to Weld and Larimer counties in the north. Ozone levels are expected to peak between noon and 10 p.m. each day, with air quality index values potentially reaching the range considered unhealthy for sensitive groups.

Health officials advised active adults, children, and people with respiratory conditions such as asthma to avoid strenuous outdoor activity during the advisory period. Residents can reduce emissions contributing to ground-level ozone by working from home, skipping commutes, or using public transportation, officials said.

stories.headline: Six wildfires burning across Colorado as fire season intensifies statewide
stories.summary:
Six active wildfires are burning across Colorado as of Thursday, with blazes scattered from Montrose County in the west to Eagle County and the southeastern plains near Trinidad. None of the fires were immediately threatening populated areas at time of reporting, though officials cautioned conditions can change rapidly.

The Paradox Fire north of Paradox in Montrose County and the nearby Beehive Fire west of Montrose both ignited June 8 following a lightning storm. Both are burning in rugged terrain and relying heavily on aerial resources for suppression. In Eagle County, near Thomasville and Ruedi Reservoir, an evacuation order remains in place as fire activity is expected to increase Thursday afternoon.

A fire near Highway 350 between Trinidad and La Junta that started June 10 prompted evacuations, which were lifted around 2:30 p.m. Thursday after the blaze slowed. The Paradise Fire near Glenwood Springs, which briefly closed I-70 in both directions when it ignited June 9, has been contained with no further road impacts expected.

The Rifle apartment fire, previously reported, appears fully contained after spreading from a residential area to surrounding wildland and destroying an eight-unit apartment building. The string of fires comes amid ongoing drought conditions and elevated wildfire risk across the state.

stories.headline: Denver man found dead in Yellowstone Lake; case under investigation
stories.summary:
Federal officials confirmed Thursday that a Denver man was found dead in Yellowstone Lake at Yellowstone National Park on Monday. Boaters spotted the body of Brandon Rhea, 41, floating near Rock Point on the northwest shore of the lake, close to Bridge Bay.

National Park Service rangers recovered Rhea's body along with a capsized kayak and personal belongings. The National Park Service announced the identification in a Thursday news release, noting the case remains under investigation.

No further details about the circumstances surrounding Rhea's death were released. The incident is a developing story as federal investigators continue their inquiry.

stories.headline: LoDo rents now more than $12 per square foot higher than rest of downtown Denver, analysis finds
stories.summary:
A new analysis by real estate firm JLL found that Lower Downtown Denver has dramatically outpaced the rest of the central business district in commercial rents, with Class A and trophy building rents in LoDo now exceeding those in the broader downtown area by more than $12 per square foot. In 2019, that gap was less than $1 per square foot. Rents in LoDo's top buildings have climbed 32% since 2019, compared with just 7% across all of downtown.

The findings underscore a widening divide within Denver's downtown real estate market, where older buildings in less walkable areas continue to struggle with high vacancies while the mixed-use LoDo neighborhood benefits from proximity to Union Station, restaurants, entertainment and transit. Building vacancies across much of downtown have reached levels not seen since the region's oil and gas bust in the mid-1980s.

Downtown Denver Partnership president and CEO Kourtny Garrett said LoDo's performance demonstrates the strength of a mixed-use neighborhood model and is shaping the organization's broader vision for downtown revitalization. The partnership is pushing a strategy of replicating LoDo's density of amenities and walkability across a wider portion of the central business district.

"It's the scale of the separation that's opened up inside downtown once you actually put hard numbers to it," said JLL senior research director T.J. Jaroszewski. "At this point, it increasingly feels like certain parts of downtown are still competing for different tenants entirely."

stories.headline: Australian fintech Earlytrade relocates to Denver, raises $25 million to expand U.S. operations
stories.summary:
Earlytrade, a financial technology company serving construction contractors, has moved its base of operations to Denver and raised $25 million in new funding to fuel regional expansion and integrate artificial intelligence into its payment platform. The company, which originated in Australia, is positioning Denver as its U.S. headquarters as it targets growth in the North American construction finance market.

The funding round signals continued investor interest in Denver's technology sector, even as the city grapples with lagging return-to-office rates and broader economic headwinds. A separate report released this week ranked Denver last among major U.S. cities for office worker visits in May, with per-day office traffic declining while most peer cities recorded gains.

Earlytrade's arrival adds to a recent wave of fintech companies establishing or expanding operations along the Front Range, drawn by the region's talent pool and relatively lower costs compared with coastal tech hubs.

stories.headline: Denver Central Market signs 10-year lease extension as longtime vendors depart amid turnover
stories.summary:
Denver Central Market, the RiNo food hall that opened in 2016, is entering its second decade with a 10-year lease extension signed by owner Ken Wolf and a round of vendor changes as several longtime tenants depart. Wolf said nine of the market's vendors agreed to the extension, though three longtime stalls will not return as leases expire.

High Point Creamery is among the departing vendors, with its space being taken over by existing tenant Izzio Bakery, which plans an unspecified new concept in the additional stall. High Point owner Erika Thomas said she had hoped to renew but her lease did not include a renewal option, leaving the decision to Wolf, who described her as a "difficult tenant." Thomas disputed that characterization, saying her advocacy for cleanliness, safety and timely expense reconciliation was the source of friction.

The market's relationship with its landlord, national retail real estate firm Edens, has also evolved. Wolf sued Edens in 2021 and posted signs telling the firm to stay out of the market, but both parties now describe the relationship as positive ahead of the next decade of operation.

"It's going to be as vibrant for the next 10 years as it was for the last 10 years," Wolf said. The 12-stall market at 2669 Larimer St. has been a anchor of the RiNo neighborhood's food and retail scene since its opening.

stories.headline: Nuggets front office faces challenge of building depth through minimum contracts this offseason
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets' front office is navigating a constrained offseason, with limited salary cap flexibility forcing the team to identify value in the minimum contract market to build depth around its star core. The challenge reflects the broader financial reality for a team carrying large contracts for its key players while trying to remain competitive in a loaded Western Conference.

With the bulk of the roster's salary committed to established contributors, team decision-makers must rely heavily on scouting and player development to supplement the rotation through below-market deals. The approach is common for contending teams operating near or above the salary cap but leaves little margin for error in roster construction.

The Nuggets finished last season short of another title run, and the front office must balance maintaining continuity with addressing positional needs that emerged during the postseason.

stories.headline: Telluride Ski Resort expansion plans draw opposition from locals, county and environmental groups
stories.summary:
Proposed infrastructure upgrades at Telluride Ski Resort are generating significant public pushback as the U.S. Forest Service reviews comments submitted in response to a draft environmental assessment. The plans, which include modernizing Lifts 7 and 8, adding snowmaking to the Jaws Skiway, and converting the High Camp warming hut into a full-service restaurant, have drawn criticism from San Miguel County commissioners, the town of Telluride, and multiple conservation organizations.

The High Camp restaurant proposal is particularly contentious, as a nearly identical concept was halted by a lawsuit in 1999 that resulted in a binding settlement. Environmental groups including Sheep Mountain Alliance, Rocky Mountain Wild and San Juan Citizens Alliance argue the current proposal constitutes "a clear violation" of that settlement. The Forest Service's draft assessment contends the settlement applies only to a specific prior record of decision and does not bind subsequent proposals.

The town of Telluride submitted a letter supporting infrastructure modernization in principle but calling for more specific lift configurations — preferring detachable quads over fixed-grip options — and greater analysis of environmental impacts. County commissioners raised concerns about water infrastructure, construction waste, and the scope of the Lynx habitat analysis included in the draft assessment.

The debate plays out against a backdrop of strained community relations following a tumultuous winter in which the resort opened late amid a ski patrol strike accompanied by public protests, the most visible episode yet in a long-running tension between residents and California-based ownership.

joke.joke: When someone asks you, 'A penny for your thoughts,' and you put your two cents in, what happens to the other penny?

weekendEvents.title: Paul Simon at Red Rocks Amphitheatre
weekendEvents.description: Music legend Paul Simon performs songs spanning six decades on his 'A Quiet Celebration' tour, including Simon & Garfunkel classics and solo hits like 'You Can Call Me Al' and 'Bridge Over Troubled Water'.

weekendEvents.title: Red Bull Soapbox Race
weekendEvents.description: The world's wildest downhill race returns to Denver for the first time in 24 years, with teams racing homemade non-motorized soapbox cars down a winding course at Empower Field at Mile High. Teams are judged on speed, design, and showmanship.
weekendEvents.location: Empower Field at Mile High, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Parker Days Festival
weekendEvents.description: The 50th annual Parker Days Festival runs all weekend with live music on four stages, more than 200 vendor booths, family-friendly activities, kids' crafts, and a carnival with rides and food vendors.
weekendEvents.time: Fri. 1–11 p.m. (carnival), 4–11 p.m. (festival); Sat. 10 a.m.–11 p.m.; Sun. 10 a.m.–9 p.m.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Gay Men's Chorus: Madam Hair
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Gay Men's Chorus presents 'Madam Hair,' a Pride Month concert centering on the legacy of hair entrepreneur A'Lelia Walker and celebrating hair as an expression of gender identity and culture within the LGBTQIA+ community.
weekendEvents.location: King Center (Fri./Sat.), 855 Lawrence Way; Arvada Center (Sun.), 6901 Wadsworth Blvd., Arvada

weekendEvents.title: Backcountry Offroad Expo
weekendEvents.description: A weekend expo at the Douglas County Fairgrounds featuring off-road vehicles, camper vans, trailers, outdoor gear brands, food trucks, live music, and educational talks. Friday night opens with a live storytelling presentation from Peter and Kathy Holcombe about their travels across the Americas.
weekendEvents.time: Fri. doors 6:30 p.m., show 7 p.m.; Sat.–Sun. expo hours, see event site

weekendEvents.title: Freedom Plane National Tour: Documents That Forged a Nation
weekendEvents.description: History Colorado Center is one of only eight institutions nationwide displaying a collection of rare founding documents, including an original engraving of the Declaration of Independence and a secret draft printing of the U.S. Constitution with handwritten delegate notes. The exhibit closes June 14.

weekendEvents.title: Ghostbusters Free Family Film Screening
weekendEvents.description: The Historic Elitch Theatre kicks off its Free Family Film Series with a screening of Ghostbusters. Pre-show activities begin at 6:30 p.m., including a costume contest for best Ghostbuster, ghost, or '80s icon, plus a photo opportunity with the Stay Puft Marshmallow Man.
weekendEvents.location: Historic Elitch Theatre, 4600 W. 37th Pl., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Highlands Art Festival
weekendEvents.description: An outdoor juried art show at the Highlands Masonic Lodge grounds featuring fine art, fine craft, live music, and a kids mobile art studio.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Renaissance Festival Opening Weekend
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Renaissance Festival opens for its 2026 season in Larkspur, featuring entertainment, food, artisan vendors, and elaborate costumes in a recreated village setting. Advance ticket purchase is recommended.

weekendEvents.title: Worldwide Knit in Public Day at Buffalo Bill Museum
weekendEvents.description: Knitters and crocheters of all ages are invited to gather on the observation deck of the Buffalo Bill Museum to enjoy the views and work on their current yarn projects. Participants receive free museum admission.
weekendEvents.location: Buffalo Bill Museum and Grave, 987 1/2 Lookout Mountain Rd., Golden

weekendEvents.title: Kid Cudi with Big Boi and Chip Tha Ripper
weekendEvents.description: Hip-hop artist Kid Cudi headlines at The Junkyard alongside Outkast legend Big Boi and longtime collaborator Chip Tha Ripper, featuring career-defining hits and festival-level production.

topStory.headline: Texas Teen Sentenced to 35 Years in Track Meet Stabbing as Case Draws Racial Debate
topStory.summary:
Karmelo Anthony, a 19-year-old Black man from Frisco, Texas, was sentenced to 35 years in prison after a jury found him guilty of murder in the April 2025 fatal stabbing of Austin Metcalf, a fellow 17-year-old, during a high school track meet in the Frisco Independent School District. The incident began when Anthony, attending the meet as a student at Frisco Centennial High School, was repeatedly asked to leave a tent belonging to Metcalf's school, Frisco Memorial High School, during a rainy competition. Witnesses testified that Metcalf shoved Anthony before Anthony stabbed him with a pocket knife, perforating his right ventricle; Metcalf was pronounced dead after being transported to a local hospital.

The jury deliberated approximately three hours before returning a guilty murder verdict on Tuesday, rejecting a lesser manslaughter charge that carried a maximum 20-year sentence. The defense argued Anthony acted in self-defense after being physically confronted by a larger opponent, while prosecutors described the killing as senseless and deliberate. Anthony filed a notice of appeal Wednesday in Collin County court, stating he cannot afford an attorney and requesting the court appoint one; he has since been transferred to the Wallace Pack Unit near Navasota, Texas, to begin serving his sentence.

The case rapidly became a focal point for national debate over race and the justice system. Anthony's father, Andrew Anthony, publicly questioned whether the outcome would have been the same had his son been tried before a jury that included Black members, noting that while the jury included some minorities, no Black jurors were seated. Both the Anthony and Metcalf families reported receiving death threats following the verdict, with Jeff Metcalf, the victim's father, describing multiple threatening messages sent to him within a single day.

Criticism of the sentencing extended beyond the families to public figures and elected officials. Rapper Cardi B and Rep. Jasmine Crockett, D-Texas, both argued that race played a role in the verdict, with Crockett stating she believed a white defendant claiming fear of a Black victim would have been treated differently by the all-white jury. Despite the prosecution asserting in opening statements that the case had nothing to do with race, and Metcalf's father disavowing those who injected racial politics into the proceedings, the case has continued to generate intense debate about self-defense standards, jury composition, and the treatment of Black defendants in the Texas legal system.
